chapters.title: AI's blind spot
chapters.text:
Be honest. Are you one of those people who always say please and thank you to ChatGPT just in case it takes over the world. If yes, there is no shame in that, since the advancement of AI technologies is so remarkable that messing with it does seem like a terrible idea. Right now, AI tools can do almost everything, from writing an essay to performing complicated data analysis to creating code. AI can also beat the best players in chess, but surprisingly, and here humanity can breathe a sigh of relief, it cannot load the dishwasher.

For us, this simple task might seem like a no-brainer, but it is an intellectual job for AI. Artificial intelligence must mimic the human brain to perform it, and here comes the first roadblock. With billions of neurons and connections, our brain is like an impenetrable fortress, and we are far from revealing all its secrets.

The brain's inner workings are your superpower, setting you apart in the digital era.

If the brain's capacity piques your interest, let us start with the basics. There were times when intelligence was not that sophisticated. To grasp its evolution, you must go back millions of years. This summary will become your time machine.

chapters.title: Left, right, and smarter
chapters.text:
Though the history of brain evolution is vast, you can distill it down to five significant breakthroughs that ultimately led to our intelligence. Breakthrough number 1 has a lot to do with steering, and to witness it, we should travel back 550 million years ago to the era of microorganisms. Just like now, animals moved around because it helped them flee from predators or, in contrast, catch their prey. For movement, they engaged their body symmetry, which fell into two categories: radial symmetry and bilateral symmetry.

Animals with radial symmetry had only one opening through which they consumed food and discarded waste. As for movement, these animals simultaneously used their sensory mechanisms to detect food and their motor system to move toward it in any direction. It was pretty energy-consuming, so they preferred staying in one place and waiting until food found them. Bilaterians were the opposite. Their bodies had two openings, and their motor system was simple, so they could only move left or right. That is why they actively steered in their surroundings, looking for food. Last but not least, only animals with bilateral symmetry developed a brain.

A microorganism called nematode was one of the first bilaterian organisms ever in the world. It could not see or distinguish what was happening around it, but its brain told it where to steer, away from light, cold, and heat and closer to food. That set them apart from radial animals and made steering the first breakthrough in the evolution of intelligence. Bilaterians actively explored the world and, by doing so, stimulated neurons and muscle cells.

Such a breakthrough did not go unnoticed by scientists, and in 2002, Rodney Brooks introduced the first successful vacuum-cleaner robot, Roomba. This robot mimicked the steering of the first bilaterians. It had a straightforward system, hardly any sensors, and could move forward and backward only, yet it was very efficient. Nature proved to be the best inspiration.

Outstanding achievements start with mastering the basics. Simplicity leads to success.

chapters.title: From instinct to insight
chapters.text:
Now, let us leave the era of microorganisms and jump forward around 50 million years. Welcome to the Cambrian period, when the animal world became very diverse, but probably not to the point you would like. Gigantic spiders and insects with claws and armored shells were casually roaming around. In this big chaos of creatures, look for the small animals that resembled fish. These were vertebrates, the ancestors of our intelligent brain. What made them notable was their vertebral column, the bones that protected the spinal column, and, of course, a more sophisticated brain with six main parts.

How did this upgrade happen? To keep the intrigue, let us first experiment with fish, the direct descendants of vertebrates. Fish have an unenviable reputation. In popular culture, they appear unintelligent. But if you were to put fish into a tank with some transparent walls inside and hidden holes to escape, then turn on the light, since fish do not like it, through trial and error, fish would find the holes and flee to the darker parts of the tank. The stereotype is no longer valid precisely because of breakthrough number 2, which is reinforcement learning in vertebrates.

To thrive in an environment, it is essential to be adaptable and embrace its unique conditions and challenges.

With reinforcement learning came other evolutionary tools, such as dopamine release, curiosity, the perception of time, the perception of three-dimensional space, and pattern recognition. Ancient bilaterians also released dopamine, but for them, this release was the sign of something good nearby, like food. For vertebrates, however, dopamine release helped to calculate the possibility of finding food. Vertebrates realized that the more they explored the world around them, the more chances they had to survive and receive rewards. They knew precisely when to act, could recognize where they were in space and differentiate themselves from the world, and could remember dangerous smells in the ocean and then apply this knowledge later to escape.

It was the first time that a brain constructed an internal model, a representation of the external world, as Max Bennett has noted.

chapters.title: Evolution's great gamble
chapters.text:
The Cambrian period is over. Now we move to the Devonian one, around 420 to 375 million years ago. This brought much destruction. The Devonian period was the time when plants finally stepped into the game. They started as tiny things around 30 centimeters and ended up as giants of 30 meters. Plants released so much oxygen and absorbed so much carbon dioxide that oceans froze, and many species were lost forever.

Those who did not die adapted to new life conditions. Kudos to our ancestors because they decided to hold on for dear life, crawled out of the water, and developed lungs and gills to be comfortable in the ocean and on land. Later, they lost their gills and became something resembling a modern salamander. Their name was tetrapods.

Life is like a river. It constantly moves and reshapes itself. Allow yourself to flow with its currents.

There was no happy ending for tetrapods and their successors, therapsids, at least for most. Another mass extinction followed, killing almost 70 percent of land animals and 96 percent of sea creatures. Our surviving ancestors did so because of their size. They were small and resembled mice or squirrels. Though small, these animals had a superpower. Their brain finally developed a neocortex.

Previously, vertebrates could learn about their surroundings by doing and trying things. With the neocortex, however, animals could finally imagine things before they acted them out. A tiny mouse sitting in a bush and spying on an insect could think through all the possible scenarios in its head and pick the best path to its prey. This simulation was breakthrough number 3. Along with it, episodic memory appeared. Now, animals could use their recollections to avoid making mistakes.

A useful side note: visually impaired people cannot send sensory signals to the neocortex, which makes it very unstable. Hence, some people struggle with Charles Bonnet syndrome, or in other words, hallucinations.

chapters.title: Crisis breeds champions
chapters.text:
If you think mass extinctions are enough for now, you are wrong. Ahead of us is the deadliest one, which happened around 60 million years ago. This time, the universe intervened. An asteroid hit the earth, destroying many species, including legendary dinosaurs, and black clouds covered the Sun for 100 years. When the Sun finally came out, the world was different. Out of all dinosaur species, only birds survived. Mammals, now without any competition, could finally reign on earth. And so they did, by diversifying their species and settling on new territories.

When life opens the door of opportunity for you, be prepared to seize it and make the most of it.

Our direct ancestors were in Africa, and they were the first primates who preferred to live in the trees. They developed much bigger brains, possibly because they lived in groups. Why was the social factor so important? Many animals lived in groups because that minimized the risk of predators attacking. Primates bonded for the same reasons, and while living together, they formed essential abilities, such as understanding each other's intentions, imitation learning, and anticipating future needs.

Primates could guess when someone was trying to trick or hide food from them. Scientists call this act the theory of mind, which is getting to understand the mind of the other creature. Primates could observe others performing actions and, by doing so, learn a new skill. On the contrary, if a chimp had never observed someone cracking nuts by age five, they would never have understood how to do it. Primates understood they had to prepare for fruit foraging, so they would start their journey early to avoid competition or sleep en route.

All these three advancements in the brain are part of breakthrough number 4, or mentalizing. It happened because primates had to put themselves in imagined future situations and try to decipher the minds of other primates.

chapters.title: Chatter that mattered
chapters.text:
For a very long time, people have considered themselves a superior species. Partially, this is true. Just look at human achievements in science, medicine, or aviation. But if we get inside our heads, our brains are no different from those of primates. So, no breakthrough number 5? Quite the opposite. There is one thing that makes us stand out, and that is communication.

Animals communicate too. Chimpanzees gesture to each other. Fish use electric signals. Bees dance to show others where food is. Humans differ in communication from all these species not because of what they communicate but because of how they do it. We use language where we apply declarative labeling or symbols. For example, a teacher at school points at objects and assigns them a name, like tree, dog, or fruit. Another thing that makes our communication so sophisticated is grammar.

But why were we only given the gift of language to communicate with each other? What might have happened during the evolutionary path? At that time, our ancestors in Africa, still in their primate form, left their forest environment due to climate change and, as a result, turned to eating meat. And then came the twist. Primate bodies were not used to eating such harsh food, so they had to use tools they made with their hands.

Eating meat and tool-making turned our ancestors into a completely new species, Homo erectus. Their legs were much longer, and they also lost hair on their bodies. Though not 100 percent sure, some scientists believe that Homo erectus spoke the protolanguage. They used it mainly to show their children how to make tools to eat food. Surprisingly, they leveled up the protolanguage when they started to gossip with each other.

Equipped with knowledge, you can turn challenges into growth opportunities.

What did ancient people gossip about? It was pretty much about the same things as we do. They shared information about group members who did something wrong and deserved punishment. The more they spoke, the more their brains evolved, resulting in better hunting and cooking skills. Evolution was accelerating, and Homo sapiens were already in the making.

Language, however, is only valuable if a group of individuals are using it, as Max Bennett has observed.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If you think this is the end of the intelligence journey, you are wrong. Life on earth took four billion years to transform from the simplest microorganisms into complex animals and humans, and we still have seven billion years before the Sun dies to advance even further. What could happen during this time? Will breakthrough number 6 be artificial superintelligence? Only time will tell.

Try this. If you are interested in delving further into the field of biology, give the Nibble app a try. Explore the Future of the Mind summary on the Headway app to learn more about what lies ahead for us in the following years. Stimulate your brain by learning a new hobby, engaging in strategy and memory games, and reading regularly.

Read it till the end?
